title: Jerk chicken
subtitle: Scotch-bonnet-and-allspice marinade, charred and smoky
excerpt: Chicken pieces marinated overnight in scotch bonnet, allspice, thyme, and spring onion, then grilled or oven-roasted until the skin blisters and chars. The defining dish of Jamaican cooking.
sourceNotes: Jerk seasoning originates with the Maroon communities of Jamaica, who preserved wild boar by rubbing it with allspice and chillies. The technique was later extended to chicken, pork, and fish. Helen Willinsky's Jerk: Barbecue from Jamaica (1990) and Levi Roots's writings document the scotch-bonnet-and-allspice profile as the canonical standard. This version follows the Jamaican home-cook tradition: wet paste rather than dry rub, spring onion as the aromatic base, overnight marinating.

content.text: Jerk chicken is the smell of roadside pits across Kingston and Montego Bay, but it works just as well in a hot oven. The key is the paste: scotch bonnet for heat, allspice for the deep, clove-like warmth, spring onion for the sharp green note, and thyme for fragrance. The overnight marinade is not negotiable for full flavour, though four hours will do in a pinch.

Jerk is properly done over pimento wood on a covered pit, which gives a smokiness no domestic oven can replicate. A charcoal barbecue is the best domestic approximation. In the oven, high heat and a good sear in the last ten minutes are what get the charred, sticky surface. Use bone-in, skin-on pieces; boneless chicken dries out too fast.

items.name: Scotch bonnet
items.prepNote: 1 for mild heat, 3 for authentic Jamaican level

content.text: chillies (halved, seeds left in), {{spring-onion}} spring onions, {{garlic}} of garlic, {{ginger-root}} of ginger, {{thyme-fresh}} of thyme leaves, {{allspice}} of ground allspice, {{cinnamon-ground}} of cinnamon, {{nutmeg-ground}} of nutmeg, {{dark-brown-sugar}} of dark brown sugar, {{soy-sauce-dark}} of soy sauce, {{vegetable-oil}} of oil, and the juice of {{lime}} limes into a food processor. Add {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt. Blitz to a coarse paste. Taste. It should be intensely fragrant, fiery, and slightly sweet.

Score the chicken thighs and drumsticks deeply through to the bone with a sharp knife, three or four cuts per piece. This lets the marinade penetrate the thickest parts and helps the skin char properly. Rub the jerk paste all over and into every cut. Transfer to a dish, cover, and refrigerate for at least 4 hours or overnight.

Heat the oven to 200°C fan. Arrange the chicken pieces skin-side up in a single layer in a roasting pan. Roast for 40 minutes, then increase the heat to 220°C for the final 10 minutes to char and caramelise the surface. The chicken is ready when a probe thermometer inserted into the thickest part of the thigh reads 75°C and the juices run clear. Rest for 10 minutes before serving.

items.fix: Start at 180°C and increase to 200°C for the final 10 minutes. Alternatively, place a sheet of foil loosely over the pan for the first 30 minutes, then remove to char the surface
items.cause: Oven too hot for the full cooking time; sugar in the marinade caramelises fast
items.symptom: Paste burning in the pan before the chicken is cooked through

items.fix: Pat the marinated chicken dry before placing in the pan. Use the grill setting for the final 5 minutes. Leave space between pieces so steam escapes
items.cause: Too much moisture on the surface; oven not hot enough; pan overcrowded

items.fix: Use 1 scotch bonnet and replace the other with a milder red chilli. Serve with rice and peas and a cooling side of sliced cucumber in lime juice to balance
items.cause: Full 2 scotch bonnets is very hot
items.symptom: Heat too intense for the table

items.fix: Score all the way to the bone. Reduce the oil by half if the paste looks loose. It should coat the chicken thickly, not run off
items.cause: Skin not scored deeply enough; too much oil in the paste
items.symptom: Paste not clinging to the chicken

content.text: Roadside jerk stalls in Jamaica cook over pimento wood in long, lidded oil-drum pits. The smoke permeates everything. That specific flavour belongs to the pit. What you can capture at home is the marinade's depth and the blistered, sticky surface. Serve with rice and peas, fried plantain, and a few slices of lime. The chicken reheats well and is if anything better the next day, cold from the fridge with the skin still tacky.
